attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man presents with a six-month history of left flank pain and generalised abdominal discomfort. He has had general malaise and 1-2kg of unintentional weight loss over the last two months. He has no haematuria or lower urinary tract symptoms. He is currently self-employed as a livestock farmer, mainly cattle and sheep. He reports episodic binge drinking with his mates on weekends.

On examination, his blood pressure is 132/78 mmHg, pulse 92/min and temperature 37.2oC. The rest of the physical examination is unremarkable apart from some mild left costophrenic angle tenderness.

A complete blood examination and metabolic panel, renal function, as well as fasting blood sugar and lipids are within normal limits. Urinalysis is within normal limits. A CT scan is arranged (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The CT scan shows a 15cm lesion in the left upper quadrant (Image). There are some small areas of mural calcification. The lesion is homogeneous, and its density is 23 Hounsfield units (meaning cystic consistency). The lesion is situated between the spleen, stomach, and kidney but is separated from these respective organs meaning the anatomical origin is most likely to have originated from the adrenal gland. The radiographic findings paired suggest an adrenal cyst is the most likely diagnosis. Adrenal cysts typically present with abdominal pain or a palpable mass.

Other options:

  • Gastrointestinal stromal tumour (GIST): GISTs are rare neoplasms that represent approximately 1-2% of primary gastrointestinal (GI) cancers, with a median age of diagnosis of 65-69 years old. The presentation of these tumours depends on the tumour’s location (i.e., upper vs lower GI) and can include GI bleeding, dysphagia, constipation, bowel obstruction, or an asymptomatic abdominal mass found on examination. Diagnosis is supported by radiographic findings consisting of a solid, smoothly contoured mass that enhances brightly with contrast.

  • Pancreatic pseudocyst: These are encapsulated, pancreatic fluid collections that occur outside the pancreas with a well-defined wall. Clinical presentations may vary from asymptomatic, severe abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and jaundice. These lesions are commonly due to progressive ductal obstruction in the setting of chronic pancreatitis.

    • This CT scan clearly shows a cystic structure that is separated from the pancreas.

  • Gastric outlet obstruction (GOO): 50-80% of all cases of GOOs are attributed to malignancy (i.e., pancreatic adenocarcinoma, distal gastric cancer). The most common features of GOO are epigastric pain, early satiety, nausea and vomiting, weight loss, and abdominal distension. Physical examination may reveal signs of malnutrition or volume depletion and/or a succussion splash. Laboratory findings can include electrolyte abnormalities like hypokalaemia or a hypochloraemic metabolic alkalosis.

  • Renal cyst: These are a common finding on abdominal imaging and are typically discovered incidentally; accounting for 65-70% of kidney masses identified on imaging. Most simple renal cysts are managed with reassurance and, in some instances, periodic surveillance.

    • This CT scan shows a lesion that is well demarcated and clearly separate from the left kidney.
learningPoints: Cysts are commonly identified in the modern era, as imaging studies have become a mainstay in patient evaluation.
references: Wedmid A, Palese M. Diagnosis and Treatment of the Adrenal Cyst. Current Urology Reports 2010;11(1):44–50. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20425637/

Howell, DA. 2021, Approach to walled-off pancreatic fluid collections in adults, UpToDate. [online]. Available at: < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/approach-to-walled-off-pancreatic-fluid-collections-in-adult>
Gan, SI. 2021. Gastric outlet obstruction in adults, UpToDate. [online]. Available at: < https://www.uptodate.com/contents/gastric-outlet-obstruction-in-adults>

Morgan, J., Raut, C., Duensing, A, Keedy, V., 2021. Clinical presentation, diagnosis, and prognosis of gastrointestinal stromal tumors, UpToDate. [online]. Available at: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/clinical-presentation-diagnosis-and-prognosis-of-gastrointestinal-stromal-tumors>
Kruskal, JB. PR, Jerome,. Simple and complex kidney cysts in adults [Internet]. Uptodate.com. 2021. Available from: <https://www.uptodate.com/contents/simple-and-complex-kidney-cysts-in-adults>
